deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered.

In transcripts 1 and 2, when students successfully articulate the means/side-effect distinction after earlier confusion, the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the new framing worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well across all three transcripts, consistently using Socratic questioning to guide students through act consequentialism and the means/side-effect distinction without giving away answers. Key ideas remain student-generated, scaffolding builds progressively on student responses, and the tutor redirects confusion (e.g., Fuel) with targeted structural questions rather than direct answers. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after students successfully improve their reasoning.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate the key distinctions (means vs. side effect, general rule formulation) before confirming or probing further. 1.2: Scaffolding is strong throughout—the tutor builds on each student's prior step, uses targeted diagnostic questions when students are confused about Fuel, and avoids method dumps. 1.3: Two deductions apply. First, across all three conversations the tutor never explains why it is asking questions rather than providing answers, missing the rationale requirement (1.3.A.a). Second, when students in transcripts 1 and 2 successfully articulate the means/side-effect rule after earlier confusion, the tutor moves directly to the next challenge without pausing to ask what changed in the student's thinking (1.3.C.a). 1.3.B is not penalized because the tutor does give process-level feedback (e.g., 'you've stated the distinction, not defended it'). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong with no evidence of repetition, drift, monologuing, or tone issues.
